comments.text: This should be accessible with sieve methods (Maynard/Chen-style). I did some back and forth with GPT-5.2 and here's where's the difficulty lies. First of all note that if $n+1$ is composite then one may take $k=1$, since $p(n+1)\geq 2>1=k^2$. Thus the only potentially difficult values are $n=p-1$ with $p$ prime. Any admissible witness must satisfy $k^4<n+k$, hence $k\leq n^{1/4}+O(1)$, and it is natural to search on a dyadic block $K<k\leq 2K$ with $K=n^\alpha$ and $\alpha<1/4$, so that $k^2\leq 4K^2$. Fix $\alpha\in(0,1/4)$, put $K=n^\alpha$ and $y=4K^2$, and consider the short interval $I=(n+K,n+2K]$. A sufficient condition is the existence of a composite $m\in I$ with $p(m)>y$ (then $m=n+k$ for some $k\in(K,2K]$ and $p(m)>y\geq k^2$). The sieve heuristic for $y$-rough numbers predicts $\#\{m\in I:p(m)>y\}\approx K\,\omega(u)/\log y$ with $u=\log n/\log y$ and Buchstab function $\omega$, while $\#\{m\in I:m\ \textrm{prime}\}\approx K/\log n$; since $u\omega(u)>1$ for $u>2$ (equivalently $\alpha<1/4$), the heuristic main term already suggests that $I$ should contain composite $y$-rough values. However, existing pointwise sieve methods do not currently yield such a uniform lower bound in a single length-$K$ interval when $y\asymp K^2$, because the lower linear sieve requires sieve level $R>y^2$ whereas one only has trivial remainder control for divisibility in a fixed short interval. Unconditionally, an averaging-over-$p$ substitute appears accessible: one may sieve the primes $p\in[X,2X]$ with $K=X^\alpha$ and $y=4K^2$ using Rosser--Iwaniec linear sieve weights and the Bombieri--Vinogradov theorem (level $1/2$) to obtain a positive lower bound for the mean of $R(p)=\#\{K<k\leq 2K:(p-1+k,P(y))=1\}$ provided $s=(\log R)/(\log y)>2$. Taking $R=X^{1/4-\varepsilon}$ and $y=X^{2\alpha}$ gives $s\approx 1/(8\alpha)$, so $s>2$ holds for any fixed $\alpha<1/16$, and a standard second-moment argument (with singular series averaging as in Gallagher) then yields $R(p)\gg K/\log X$ for all but $o(\pi(X))$ primes $p\in[X,2X]$. Since primes in $(p+K,p+2K]$ contribute only $\asymp K/\log X$, one obtains for almost all such primes $p$ a shift $k\in(K,2K]$ with $p-1+k$ composite and $p(p-1+k)>k^2$. References for the sieve ingredients include J. Friedlander and H. Iwaniec, Opera de Cribro (2010), and D. R. Heath-Brown, Lectures on sieves (2002); improving the exponent toward $\alpha=1/4$ would require new distribution input beyond Bombieri--Vinogradov.
A389680 is now published in OEIS (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
In case you are not aware, it is possible to add OEIS sequences directly to the database yourself. See here.
